title: Estimate Sums
essential_question: How can you use compatible numbers and rounding to estimate sums?

expected_errors.note: A: 38→30 — ones=8 should round up to 40; student rounds down; 30+20=50

expected_errors.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.37-38 — systematic rounding bias patterns.
expected_errors.note: C: 21→30 — ones=1 should round down to 20; rounds up; 40+30=70

pretest.hint: Round each number to the nearest ten first, then add.

pretest.question: Use compatible numbers to estimate. 48 + 23 = ?

expected_errors.note: B: 50+25=75 — reasonable compatible pair but non-standard choice

expected_errors.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.37-38 — systematic rounding bias patterns.
expected_errors.note: C: 48→40, 23→20 — both rounded down; 40+20=60

expected_errors.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.37-38 — systematic rounding bias patterns.
expected_errors.note: D: 23→30 — rounds up when 23<25 midpoint; 50+30=80

solution_steps: Find compatible numbers close to 48 and 23
The actual sum is 71, so 70 is close
pretest.hint: Compatible numbers are numbers that are easy to add mentally.

learn.title: What Is Estimating?
learn.content: Sometimes you don't need an exact answer. An estimate is a number close to the exact answer. When you estimate a sum, you find about how much two numbers add up to.

visual_data.description: A child at a store with items priced $3.85 and $5.12. Thought bubble: About $4 + $5 = about $9. I have $10, so I have enough!

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

learn.title: Method 1: Rounding
learn.content: To estimate a sum by rounding: 1) Round each number to the same place (nearest ten or nearest hundred). 2) Add the rounded numbers. Example: 367 + 512. Round to hundreds: 400 + 500 = 900. So 367 + 512 is about 900.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

learn.title: Method 2: Compatible Numbers
learn.content: Compatible numbers are numbers that are easy to compute mentally. They are close to the actual numbers but simpler. Example: 367 + 512. Use compatible numbers: 375 + 525 = 900. So 367 + 512 is about 900.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

learn.title: Rounding Two-Digit Numbers to Estimate
learn.content: For two-digit numbers, round to the nearest ten. Example: 27 + 78. Round: 30 + 80 = 110. So 27 + 78 is about 110. The exact answer is 105. The estimate 110 is close!

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

learn.title: Rounding Three-Digit Numbers to Estimate
learn.content: For three-digit numbers, round to the nearest hundred. Example: 186 + 460. Round: 200 + 500 = 700. So 186 + 460 is about 700. The exact answer is 646. The estimate 700 is a good approximation.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

learn.title: Choosing the Best Method
learn.content: Both rounding and compatible numbers give good estimates. Sometimes one method gives a closer estimate than the other. Example: 278 + 369. Rounding: 300 + 400 = 700. Compatible: 275 + 325 = 600. Exact: 647. Here, compatible numbers (600) are closer to the exact answer (647).

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

learn.title: Using Estimation in Word Problems
learn.content: Estimation helps when you need a quick answer. Example: A store sold 238 pet bowls in June and 345 in July. About how many were sold in both months? Round: 200 + 300 = 500. About 500 pet bowls were sold.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

learn.math_talk_prompt: Explain your estimation strategy: Did you use rounding or compatible numbers? Which method gives a closer estimate here, and how do you know without finding the exact answer?

learn.title: Key Idea Summary
learn.content: To estimate sums: Rounding — round each number to the nearest ten or hundred, then add. Compatible numbers — replace each number with a close number that is easy to add. Both methods give a number close to the exact answer. Use the word 'about' when you estimate.

practice_r2.hint: Round each to the nearest ten.

practice_r2.question: A bakery made 389 muffins on Saturday and 412 muffins on Sunday. About how many muffins were made in all?

expected_errors.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.37-38 — systematic rounding bias patterns.
expected_errors.note: A: 389→300 — tens=8 should round up to 400; rounds down; 300+400=700

expected_errors.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.37-38 — systematic rounding bias patterns.

practice_r3.question: A pet store sold 238 pet bowls in June, 345 in July, and 413 in August. About how many pet bowls were sold in all three months?

expected_errors.note: A: rounding gives 200+300+400=900 — valid method; compatible (1,000) is closer to actual 996

expected_errors.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.37-38 — systematic rounding bias patterns.
expected_errors.note: C: 238→300 — tens=3 should give 200; rounds up; 300+400+400=1,100
D: only two months added; operation error

practice_r3.hint: Round each number, then add all three.

practice_r3.question: Sam added 483 + 326 and got 709. He estimates to check: 500 + 300 = 800. Is Sam's answer reasonable?

choices.A: No, his answer seems too low — the estimate is 800 but he got 709
choices.C: No, because he should get exactly 800
choices.D: Yes, because both numbers round up

expected_errors.note: B: accepts 709 as reasonable vs estimate 800; misunderstands acceptable gap
C: expects estimate to equal exact answer; concept gap about estimation precision
D: incorrectly states both numbers round up; 326 rounds down to 300

solution_steps: The gap between 709 and 800 is 91 — that's large
Sam likely made a subtraction error (forgot to regroup). His answer is NOT reasonable.
practice_r3.hint: If the estimate and the answer are very far apart, something may be wrong.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

verification.math_note: Exact: 483+326=809. Alex's 709 is off by 100 from estimate 800 — NOT reasonable. ✓

practice_r3.question: Julia says 'compatible numbers always give a closer estimate than rounding.' Is she correct?

choices.A: Yes, compatible numbers are always better
choices.B: No, sometimes rounding gives a closer estimate
choices.C: Yes, because compatible numbers are exact
choices.D: No, both methods always give the same estimate

expected_errors.note: A: accepts Julia's claim without testing a counterexample; concept gap
C: believes compatible numbers produce exact answers; concept gap
D: assumes both methods always yield the same estimate; concept gap

solution_steps: But it depends on the numbers. Julia is not always correct.
practice_r3.hint: Try both methods with the same numbers and compare.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

verification.math_note: Counter: 465+278=743. Rounding: 800 (off 57). Compatible 475+275=750 (off 7). Julia is wrong. ✓

practice_r3.question: A school needs 500 chairs for an event. They have 267 chairs in the gym and 189 in the cafeteria. Without finding the exact total, do they have enough chairs?

choices.D: It's too close to tell by estimating alone

expected_errors.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.37-38 — systematic rounding bias patterns.
expected_errors.note: A: rounds both up: 300+200=500; concludes enough, but actual 456 < 500

expected_errors.citation: Ashlock (2010) p.37-38 — systematic rounding bias patterns.
expected_errors.note: B: rounds aggressively down: 200+100=300; underestimates
C: claims 267+189>500; arithmetic error (267+189=456)

solution_steps: Estimates disagree — can't be sure without exact math
practice_r3.hint: Try rounding up AND down. Do both give the same conclusion?

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

practice_r3.question: Alex estimated 678 + 215 by rounding: '700 + 200 = 800.' His friend Amy used compatible numbers: '675 + 225 = 900.' Who has a better estimate?

choices.A: Alex, because 800 is closer to the exact answer
choices.B: Amy, because 900 is closer to the exact answer

expected_errors.note: A: 800 is off by 93 from 893; 900 is off by 7; comparison error
C: 93 ≠ 7; estimates are not equally close
D: both used valid methods; labelling them errors is a concept gap

solution_steps: Alex said 700+200=800 (but 700+200 actually = 900, so Alex also made an arithmetic error)
893 is much closer to 900 (off by 7) than to 800 (off by 93)
practice_r3.hint: Calculate the exact sum, then see which estimate is closest.

concept_source.name: Illustrative Mathematics G3 Unit 4 Lesson 2 — Estimating to Check Answers

procedure_source.name: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 2 Topic A Lesson 3 — Rounding to Estimate Sums

assessment_source.name: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Sample Items — 3.NBT.A.1 Estimation

verification.math_note: 678+215=893. Alex stated 700+200=800 (arithmetic error in problem; 700+200=900). Amy: 675+225=900. 893 closer to 900 (off 7) than 800 (off 93). Amy better. ✓

unit_intro_message: 이 단원에서는 1,000까지의 수를 더하고 빼는 여러 전략을 배웁니다. 어림(estimation), 수 감각(number sense), 자리값(place value) 이해가 핵심입니다.
unit_close_message: 잘했어요! 오늘 배운 어림 전략을 실생활 문제에 적용해 보세요. 다음 차시에서는 반올림을 이용한 덧셈을 더 연습합니다.
